verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And Stephen, full of faith and power, did great wonders and miracles among the people.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how even among the seven one was preëminent, and won the first prize.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For though the ordination was common to him and them, yet he drew upon himself greater grace.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe, how he wrought no (signs and wonders) before this time, but only when he became publicly known; to show that grace alone is not sufficient, but there must be ordination also; so that there was a further access of the Spirit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if they were full of the Spirit, it was of that which is from the Laver of Baptism.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then there arose certain of them of the synagogue.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again he uses the phrase of “rising up” (ἀνάστασιν, Hom. xiii. p.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 81), to denote their exasperation and wrath.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here we have a great multitude.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe the difference in the form of accusation: for since Gamaliel had stopped them from finding fault on the former plea, they bring in another charge.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And there rose up, it says, certain of them of the synagogue of those who are called (τὥν λεγομένων.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Edd. τἥς λεγομένης) Libertines, and of the Cyrenians and Alexandrians, and of them of Cilicia and Asia, disputing with Stephen.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they were not able to resist the wisdom and the spirit by which he spake.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then they suborned men, which said, We have heard him speak blasphemous words against Moses, and against God.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That they may establish the charge, the phrase is, “he speaks against God, and against Moses.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And with this object too they disputed, that they might force him to say somewhat.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But he now discoursed more openly, and perhaps spoke of the cessation of the Divine Law: or, spoke it not, but hinted as much: since had he spoken plainly, there had been no need of suborned men, nor yet of false witnesses.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The synagogues were diverse: [to wit, “Of the Libertines”]: “of the Cyrenians, i.e. those in the parts beyond Alexandria [“of the Alexandrians,” etc.].

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There also they seem to have had synagogues according to their different nations; for many stayed behind there, that they might not be obliged to be continually travelling.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The Libertines perhaps were freedmen of the Romans.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As there were many foreigners dwelling there, so they had their synagogues, where the Law was to be read.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Disputing with Stephen.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe him, not taking upon him to teach, but forced to do so.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The miracles once more brought him into ill-will; but when he overcame in argument, it was false-witness!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For they did not wish to kill intolerable to them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “They could not resist, etc.: then they suborned men.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Everywhere out of hand, but by means of a sentence, that they might hurt their reputation also: and leaving those (the Apostles), they attack these (the disciples), thinking in this way to terrify those also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They say not, “he speaketh,” but, “he ceaseth not to speak.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they stirred up the people, and the elders, and the scribes, and came upon him, and caught him, and brought him to the council, and set up false witnesses, which said, This man ceaseth not to speak blasphemous words against this holy place, and the law.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Ceaseth not,” say they, as if he made this his business.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For we have heard him say that this Jesus of Nazareth shall destroy this place, and shall change the customs which Moses delivered us.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Jesus,” they say, “the Nazarene,” as a term of reproach, “shall destroy this place, and shall change the customs.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is also what they said about Christ.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Thou that destroyest this Temple.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Matt. xxvii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For great was their veneration for the Temple (as indeed they had chosen to leave their own country (μετοικεἴν) in order to be near it) and for the name of Moses.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The charge is twofold.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If He “shall change the customs,” He will also introduce others instead: observe how the charge is a bitter one, and fraught with perils.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And all that sat in the council, looking steadfastly on him, saw his face as it had been the face of an angel.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So possible is it even for one in a lower degree to shine.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For what, I ask, had this man less than the Apostles?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He lacked not miracles, and great was the boldness he exhibited.—“They saw His face,” it is said, “as it had been the face of an angel.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For this was his grace, this was the glory of Moses.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: God made him thus gracious (ἐπίχαριν) of visage, now that he was about to say somewhat, thus at once by his very look to awe them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For there are, yes, there are faces full-fraught with spiritual grace, lovely to them that love, awful to haters and enemies.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It mentions also the reason, why they suffered his oration.—“Then,” it proceeds, “said the high-priest, Are these things so?” (ch. vii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe, the question is put with mildness, that he may effect some great mischief.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For this reason Stephen too begins his speech in a tone of gentleness, and says, “Men, brethren, and fathers, hearken; The God of glory appeared unto our father Abraham, when he was in Mesopotamia, before he dwelt in Charran.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Immediately at the outset he overthrows their conceit, and makes it appear by what he says, that the temple is nothing, that the customs are nothing either, without their suspecting his drift: also that they shall not overcome the preaching; and that from powerless (ἀμηχάνων) things God evermore contrives Him powerful (εὐμήχανα) instruments.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark then how these threads make the texture of the whole speech: and moreover that having evermore enjoyed exceeding goodness, they still requited their Benefactor with the opposite conduct, and that they are now attempting impossibilities.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “The God of glory appeared unto our father Abraham, when he was in Mesopotamia, before he came into Charran.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Both the temple was not, and sacrifice was not, and yet a vision of God was vouchsafed to Abraham, and yet had he Persians for his ancestors, and was in a strange land.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he does well at the beginning of his speech to call Him, “the God of glory:” seeing that He hath made them that are without honor to be glorious.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Because” (says he) “it was He that made them glorious, He will make us also.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how he leads them away from things of the body, from the place, in the first instance, as the place was in question.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “The God of glory,” says he: implying again, that He needs not the glory which comes from us, which comes by the Temple: for Himself is the Fountain thereof.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Think not, he would say, in this way to glorify Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And from thy kindred.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How then saith the Scripture, that Abraham’s father was willing to go out?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hence we learn, that it was in consequence of Abraham’s vision, that his father was moved to join in the migration.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And said unto him, Get thee out of thy country, and from thy kindred, and come into a land which I shall show thee.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It shows how far these men are from being children of Abraham, how obedient he was.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And from thy kindred.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Uncomfortable (φορτικὰ) reflections, both, that he endured the labors, while ye reap the fruits, and that all your ancestors were in evil case.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then came he out of the land of the Chaldæans, and dwelt in Charran: and from thence, when his father was dead, He removed him into this land, wherein ye now dwell.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And He gave him none inheritance in it, no, not so much as to set his foot on.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how he raises their thoughts away from (their possession of) the land.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if He said (that, He will give: clearly [all came from him], and nothing from themselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he came, having left both kindred and country.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore then did He not give it to him?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Truly it was a figure of another land.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And He promised to give it to him.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you perceive, that he does not merely resume the thread of his discourse?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He gave him not,” says he; “and He promised; and to his seed after him, when as yet he had no child.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again, what God can do: that out of impossibilities, He doeth all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For here is a man in Persia, so far away, and this man God saith He will make lord of Palestine.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But let us look back to what was said before.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whence, I pray you, did that grace bloom upon the countenance of Stephen?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The writer gives him this report above, that he was “full of faith.” (ch. vi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it is possible to have a grace that does not consist in works of healing: “For to one is given the grace of the Spirit (1 Cor. xii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 8, 9) in such and such wise (τοιὣσδε).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But here, it seems to me, it says that he was also gracious to look at: “They saw his face as it had been the face of an angel.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Full of faith and of power”: (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 15) which is also the character given of Barnabas “he was a good man, full of faith and of the Holy Ghost.” (ch. xi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whence we learn that the sincere and innocent are, above all others, the men to be saved, and that these same are also more gracious.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then they suborned men, which said, We have heard him speak blasphemous words.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In the case of the Apostles they were annoyed that they preached the Resurrection, and that much people flowed unto them: but in this case, that they were getting their diseases healed. (ch. iv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The things for which they ought to give thanks, they made matter of blame: O the madness!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The men who overcame them by works, they expected to overcome by words!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is just what they did in the case of Christ, and always they forced them to words.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For they were ashamed to seize them without more ado, having nothing to charge them with.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe, not the persons themselves who bring them to judgment bear witness against them; for they would have been refuted: but they simply hire others, that it may not seem to be an act of mere violence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is all of a piece with their proceeding in the case of Christ.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe the power of the preaching, that, though they are not only scourged but stoned, it still prevails: not only, private individuals as they are, dragged to the bar, but assailed from all quarters: and, their enemies themselves being witnesses, not only were these worsted, but “they were not able” even “to resist” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 10), though they were exceeding shameless: so mightily did it overthrow them, for all that they could do with their preposterous figments (as the saying that He had a devil—He that cast out devils!).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the battle was not man’s, but God’s against men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And there were many combined together; not only they in Jerusalem, but others as well. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For “we have heard him,” say they, “speaking blasphemous words against Moses and against God.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: O ye shameless ones!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Ye work blasphemous deeds, and think nothing of it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is why Moses is added—because the things of God were no great concern to them: and it is ever and always Moses that they make mention of: “This Moses, which brought us out.” (ch. vii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And they stirred up the people.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Fickleness of the multitude!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet how could a man who was a blasphemer have so succeeded?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How could a blasphemer work such miracles among the people?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But the undisciplined multitude made them strong who had the worst of it (in argument).—This was what most annoyed them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “We have heard him,” they say, “speaking blasphemous words against Moses and against God” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 13): and again, “This man ceaseth not to speak blasphemous words against this holy place and the law,” and with an addition, “the customs” “which Moses delivered to us” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 14); Moses, not God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Upon the supposition of a design to overturn their manner of life (πολιτείας), they accused him of impiety also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But to show that it was not in the nature of such a man to speak such things, and harshly [“Then all,” it says, “which were in the council, looking steadfastly upon him, saw his face, as it had been the face of an angel”] (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 15): so mild was he even in countenance.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, in cases where persons were not falsely accused, Scripture mentions nothing of this kind: but as in this case it was all false accusation, with reason does God rectify it by the very look of the man.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the Apostles indeed were not falsely accused, but were forbidden: but this man is falsely accused: and therefore before all else his countenance pleads for him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This abashed even the priest.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he said,” etc. (ch. vii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He shows here, that the promise was made before the Place, before Circumcision, before Sacrifice, before the Temple, and that it was not of their merit that these received either Circumcision or Law, but that the land was the reward of obedience alone.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Moreover, that neither on the giving of circumcision does the promise receive its fulfillment.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Also, that these were figures, and (so was) both the leaving his country at God’s command—not against the law (for home and country is where God shall lead): “Then came he out,” it says, “of the land of the Chaldeans” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 4):—and that if one look closely into the matter, the Jews are of Persian origin: and that, without miracles, one must do as God bids, whatever hardships be the consequence; since the Patriarch left both the grave of his father and all that he had, in obedience to God’s command.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if Abraham’s father was not allowed to take part with him in the privilege of migrating to Palestine, because he was unworthy: much more shall the children (be excluded at last), for all that they may have gone a good distance on the way.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And He promised,” it says, “to give it to him, and to his seed after him.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Herein is shown the greatness both of God’s goodness and of Abraham’s faith.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the expression, “when as yet he had no child,” does show his obedience and faith.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Promised to give it to him and to his seed.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet the events showed the contrary: namely, after he came, he had not “so much as to set his foot on,” had not a child; which very things were contrary to his faith.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: These things having seen, let us likewise, whatever God shall promise, receive the same, however contrary may be the events.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet in our case, they are not contrary, but very suitable.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For where the promises are, there, when the contraries turn out, they are really contrary; but in our case it is just the reverse: for He has told us that we should have tribulation here, but our rest there.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why do we confound the times?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why do we turn things upside down?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say, art thou afflicted, and livest in poverty, and in dejection?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Be not troubled: for it were worth being troubled at, wert thou destined to be afflicted in that world: as for this present affliction, it is the cause of rest.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “This sickness,” saith He, “is not unto death.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That affliction is punishment: this, schooling and correction.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is a contest, this life present: if so, to fight is our business now: it is war and battle.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In war one does not seek to have rest, in war one does not seek to have dainty living, one is not anxious about riches, one’s care is not about a wife then: one thing only he looks at, how he may overcome his foes.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Be this our care likewise: if we overcome, and return with the victory, God will give us all things.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Be this alone our study, how we may overcome the devil: though after all it is not our own study that does it, but God’s grace does the whole business.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Be it our one study, how we may attract His grace, how we may draw to ourselves that assistance.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “If God be for us, who can be against us?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us make one thing our study; that He be not our enemy, that He turn not away from us.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not the being afflicted is an evil; the evil is, to sin.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is the sore affliction, however we may pass our days in luxury:—not to speak of the life to come, it is so even in this life present.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Think how our conscience is stung with remorse, and whether this is not worse than any kind of torture!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I should like to put the question searchingly to those who live in evil ways (ἐν κακοἵς), whether they never come to reflect upon their own sins, whether they do not tremble, and are in fear and anguish, whether they do not think those blessed who live in abstinence, them of the mountains, them of the strict rule? (τοὺς ἐν πολλῇ φιλοσοφί& 139·.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Dost thou wish to find rest in the life to come?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Suffer affliction in this life for Christ’s sake: there is nothing equal to this rest.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The Apostles rejoiced when scourged.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Paul gives this exhortation, saying, “Rejoice in the Lord.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Philip. iv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And how can there be rejoicing, where there are bonds, where there are tortures; where there are courts of justice?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There, most of all, is rejoicing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But say, how can there be rejoicing, where these are not?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he who is conscious of no evil, will have a sort of exceeding delight, insomuch that in what degree you speak of tribulation, in the same you tell of his delight.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The soldier who has received numberless wounds and is come home again, will he not return with exceeding delight, with his wounds as his title for speaking up boldly, and as evidence of his glory and renown?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And thou, if thou be able to exclaim as Paul does, “I bear the marks of Jesus” (Gal. vi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 17), wilt be able to become great and glorious and renowned.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But there is no persecution.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Make thy stand against glory: and should any one speak anything against thee, fear not to be evil-spoken of for Christ’s sake: make thy stand against the tyranny of pride, against the fighting of anger, against the torment of concupiscence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: These also are “marks,” these also are torments.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, I ask, what is the worst in tortures?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Is it not, that the soul is pained, and is on fire?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For in the other case, the body too has its share: but in this, the whole belongs to the soul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: On the soul alone comes all the smart, when one is angry, when one is envious, whatever else of this kind one does, or rather suffers.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, in fact, it is not action, but passion, not a doing, but a suffering—to be angered, to feel envy: therefore indeed they are called passions (or sufferings) (πάθη, perturbationes) of the soul, yea wounds, and bruises.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it is indeed a suffering, and worse than suffering.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Bethink you, ye that are angry, that ye do such things in “passion,” in a state of suffering.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore he who is not angry suffers not.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark that not he who is abused is the sufferer, but he that abuses, as I said above?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For that he is a sufferer, is plain in the first place from the very fact, that such a thing is called by this name of passion: and it is also plain from the (effects on the) body: for these are the affections (πάθη) for “sufferings,” as we call them] engendered by anger, viz. dimness of vision, insanity, and numberless others.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But he insulted my boy,” say you; “but [he called him] clown.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Deem it not weakness thy not doing the same thing thyself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, I ask you, was it well done?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: You will not say that: then leave that undone which being done were not well done.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I know what passions are engendered in such cases.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But,” say you, “how if he despise me, how if he say it again?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Show him that he is in the wrong: rebuke him, entreat him: by meekness anger is put down: go and expostulate with him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For though in cases of wrong done to ourselves it is right not to do even this, yet it is quite necessary to do it in behalf of others.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do not look on it as an insult to yourself that your boy has been insulted: annoyed you may be for his sake, yet not as if you were insulted: for it does not follow because your boy has been ill-treated, that you are disgraced, but he is disgraced that did the ill.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Quench (thine anger) that sharp sword: let it lie in its scabbard.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If we have it unsheathed, we shall be apt to use it even when the time is not proper, being drawn on by it: but if it be hidden, though a necessity should arise, yet, while we seek it in order to draw it, the anger will be quenched.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Christ would not have us be angry on his account: (hear what He saith to Peter: “Put up again thy sword into the sheath:”) (Matt. xxvi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 52) and art thou angry on account of a boy?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Teach thy boy also to be philosophical: tell him thy own sufferings: imitate (herein) thy Teacher.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Matt. xxvi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When they too (His disciples) were about to be treated with dishonor, He said not, “I will avenge you:” but, “to Me also,” saith He, “they have done the same: bear it nobly, for ye are not better than I.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: These words too do thou speak to thy son and thy boy: “Thou art not better than thy master.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But these words of philosophy are counted as the talk of a widow woman.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Alas! that it is not in the power of words to bring it home to people in the way that it is possible to be taught it by actual experience!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And that you may learn this; stand between two combatants, take part with the wronged, not with the wrong-doers [that you may learn] whether you shall not see the victory on your side, whether you shall not get splendid crowns.—See, how God is insulted, and how He answers; how gently, “Where,” saith He, “is Abel thy brother?” and what saith the other: “Am I my brother’s keeper?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What could be more contumacious than this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Would any one have heard it (patiently) even from a son? and if from a brother, would he not have thought such conduct an insult?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how again God gently answers, “The voice of thy brother’s blood,” saith He “crieth unto Me.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But God,” it will be said, “is superior to wrath.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yes, but for this reason the Son of God came down, that He might make thee a God as far as human power can go.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But I cannot,” says one, “seeing I am man.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well then, let us give you men for instances.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And do not suppose I speak of Paul or of Peter: no, but of some of inferior sort, yea, very much lower down.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Eli’s menial insulted Hannah, saying, “Put away thy wine from thee.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What could be more insulting than this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What then said she?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “I am a woman of a hard lot.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Indeed, there is nothing equal to affliction: she is the mother of true philosophy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But this same woman, though she has her rival, insulted her not: but what does she?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: She takes refuge with God, and in her prayer does not even make mention of her, nor say, “Avenge me, for such an one reproaches me:” so magnanimous was that woman (let us men be ashamed):—and yet ye know, that there is nothing like jealousy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The publican, when insulted by the Pharisee, insulted not in return, though, had he wished it, he might have done so: but he bore it like a philosopher, saying, “Be merciful to me a sinner.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Luke xviii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mephibosheth, having been accused and calumniated by his servant, neither said, nor did, any evil to him, not even in the presence of the king himself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (2 Sam. xix.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Shall I tell you even of a harlot, what philosophic magnanimity she showed?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hear Christ saying, as she was wiping His feet with her hair, “The publicans and harlots go into the kingdom before you.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you see her standing, and taking courage, and washing away her own sins?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe, how she was not angry even with the Pharisee, when reproached by him: “for had He known,” says he, “that this woman is a sinner, He would not have suffered her (Luke vii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 39): and how she said not to him, “What then?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say, art thou pure from sins?” but felt more, wept more, and let fall hotter tears.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if women and publicans and harlots play the philosopher, and that before grace (i.e. of Baptism), what pardon can they deserve, who, after so great grace, fight, and worry, and kick one another, worse than beasts?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nothing is more base than passion, nothing more disgraceful, nothing more frightful, nothing more odious, nothing more hurtful.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: These things I say, not only in order that towards men we may be gentle, but also if a wife be a talker, that thou mayest bear it: let thy wife be to thee a school for training and exercise (παλαίστρα καὶ γυμνάσιον).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For how can it but be absurd, to submit to exercises which yield no profit, where we afflict the body, but not to practise exercises at home, which, even before the contest, present to us a crown?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Does thy wife abuse thee?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do not thou become a woman: to be abusive is womanly: it is a disease of the soul, an inferiority.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Think not that it is unworthy of thee, when thy wife abuses thee.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Unworthy it is, when thou art abusive, but she bears patiently (φιλοσοφῇ): then dost thou act unseemly, then art thou disgraced: but if, having been abused, thou bear it, great is the proof of thy strength.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I do not say this, to induce wives to be abusive: God forbid: but only in case it should so happen at the instance of Satan.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is the part of men that are strong, to bear the weak.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And if thy servant contradict thee, bear it philosophically: not what he deserves to have said to him, do thou say or do, but that which it behooves thee both to do and to say.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Never insult a girl by uttering some foul word against her: never call thy servant, scoundrel (μιαρὸν): not he is disgraced, but thou.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is not possible to be master of one’s self, being in a passion.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Like a sea rolling mountains high, it is all hurly-burly: or even as a pure fountain, when mire is cast into it, becomes muddied, and all is in turmoil.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: You may beat him, you may rend his coat to rags, but it is you that sustain the greater damage: for to him the blow is on the body and the garment, but to you on the soul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is your own soul that you have cut open; it is there that you have inflicted a wound: you have flung your own charioteer from his horses, you have got him dragging along the ground upon his back.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And it is all one, as if one driver being in a passion with another, should choose to be thus dragged along.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: You may rebuke, you may chide, you may do whatever if be, only let it be without wrath and passion.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if he who rebukes is physician to him who offends, how can he heal another, when he has first hurt himself, when he does not heal himself?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say, if a physician should go to heal another person, does he first wound his own hand, first blind his own eyes, and so set about healing that other?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So also, however thou rebuke, however thou chide, let thine eyes see clearly.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do not make thy mind muddy, else how shall the cure be wrought?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is not possible to be in the same tranquillity, being in a passion, and being free from passion.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why dost thou first overturn thy master from his seat, and then discourse with him as he lies sprawling on the ground?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Seest thou not the judges, how, when about to hold the assize, they seat themselves upon the bench, in their becoming attire?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus do thou likewise dress thy soul with the judicial robe (which is gentleness).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But he will not be afraid of me,” say you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He will be the more afraid.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In the other case, though you speak justly, your servant will impute it to passion: but if you do it with gentleness, he will condemn himself: and, what is of the first importance, God will accept thee, and thus thou wilt be able to attain unto the eternal blessings, through the grace and loving-kindness of our Lord Jesus Christ, with Whom to the Father together with the Holy Spirit be glory, dominion, and honor, now and ever, and world without end.
